projects.title: ShipConnect
projects.subtitle: A logistics SaaS platform connecting startups with global shipping networks — I led the frontend for the startup-facing dashboard, authentication system, and the full onboarding flow.
projects.desc: Logistics SaaS platform built with a cross-functional team — I owned the startup dashboard, login & registration, and the complete authentication user flow.
overview: ShipConnect is a logistics SaaS platform built to bridge the gap between early-stage startups and the global shipping infrastructure they need to scale. The platform allows startup founders to register, manage shipments, track logistics operations, and integrate with carrier networks — all from a single, purpose-built dashboard.

This was a graduation-level group project built in close collaboration between a frontend pair and a backend team. My ownership was clear and scoped: I designed and built the startup-facing dashboard, the complete authentication system (login, registration, and the full connected flow), and every UI state and screen that lives between them. The design was grounded in a Figma prototype, and I translated it into a live, API-connected React application working directly with the backend team's endpoints.

challenges.challenge: Building an authentication flow that felt seamless across login, registration, and protected routes — coordinated with a live backend.
challenges.solution: Implemented JWT-based authentication with token storage and automatic refresh logic, protecting routes client-side with React Router guards while keeping the UX frictionless. Every edge case — invalid credentials, session expiry, network errors — had a deliberate UI response.

challenges.challenge: Designing a startup dashboard that surfaced the most critical logistics data without overwhelming new users.
challenges.solution: Worked from the Figma prototype to prioritize a progressive disclosure approach: key shipment metrics and status cards up front, detailed breakdowns expandable on demand. The dashboard was built to feel manageable on day one and powerful as the startup scaled.

challenges.challenge: Coordinating frontend work with a backend team building APIs in parallel, without a finalized contract.
challenges.solution: Agreed on a shared API contract early, then built against mock data while the backend was in development. Once endpoints were live, integration was a clean swap — no rewrites, no UI rework needed.

projects.title: Glamorix
projects.subtitle: A full-featured beauty e-commerce platform with seamless cart management, secure payments, and a dark/light UI that feels as refined as the products it sells.
projects.desc: A modern, responsive beauty and skincare e-commerce store — featuring authentication, cart, wishlist, PayPal checkout, and a dual-theme UI.
overview: Glamorix is a modern e-commerce platform built for beauty and skincare products — designed to feel as considered and elegant as the brands it showcases. The project covers the full customer journey: product discovery, wishlist curation, cart management, and a smooth PayPal-powered checkout.

As the frontend lead on a collaborative team, I was responsible for architecting the UI, building the component system, implementing the authentication flow, and integrating the PayPal SDK. The app supports both dark and light themes, with a responsive layout that holds up across every screen size.

challenges.challenge: Keeping cart and wishlist state consistent across pages and user sessions without a backend.
challenges.solution: Managed all client state through React context combined with localStorage persistence, ensuring continuity across refreshes and navigation without a server dependency.

challenges.challenge: Building a believable checkout experience with PayPal integration inside a mock-API environment.
challenges.solution: Wired the PayPal React SDK to a JSON Server backend, simulating real order creation and confirmation flows — giving the checkout a production-grade feel in a fully local environment.

challenges.challenge: Delivering a dual-theme UI that felt intentional rather than just toggled colors.
challenges.solution: Designed the theme system from the ground up using DaisyUI's theming layer over Tailwind, defining separate token sets for each mode — the light theme felt warm and airy, the dark theme rich and editorial.

projects.title: Design Trends
projects.subtitle: A full UI/UX case study for a personalized shopping platform — from user research and information architecture to high-fidelity Figma screens and a complete design system.
projects.desc: UI/UX case study for a personalized e-commerce platform — covering user flows, wireframes, and high-fidelity Figma screens with a full design system.
overview: Design Trends is a concept e-commerce platform designed around one central idea: shopping should feel personal, not algorithmic. The project began with a deep dive into how users actually navigate online stores — where they drop off, what earns their trust, and what pushes them to convert.

The result is a comprehensive design system and set of high-fidelity Figma screens that prioritize personalization at every touchpoint — from a smart onboarding flow that captures taste preferences, to a product page designed to reduce decision fatigue. Every component was built with reusability in mind: color tokens, documented interaction states, and an auto-layout grid that scales from mobile to desktop.

challenges.challenge: Designing a personalization experience that felt helpful rather than intrusive or surveillance-like.
challenges.solution: Introduced progressive disclosure — users set broad preferences during onboarding, and the platform refines recommendations silently based on browsing behavior. No consent fatigue, no pop-up overload.

challenges.challenge: Making a complex product filtering system feel effortless across screen sizes.
challenges.solution: Designed a persistent side-panel filter for desktop and a bottom-sheet drawer for mobile, using the same interaction logic but adapted to each context's natural gestures and thumb zones.

challenges.challenge: Building a design system flexible enough to span diverse product categories.
challenges.solution: Created a token-based color system with category-level accent overrides, so each section had its own visual mood — skincare soft, fashion bold, accessories minimal — while remaining part of one coherent brand.

projects.title: OSMAUS
projects.subtitle: A web design concept that reimagines project management as a calm, structured workspace — less noise, more focus, built for the way teams actually think.
projects.desc: Web design concept for a modern project management platform — clean architecture, purposeful hierarchy, and a UI built around individual focus rather than managerial oversight.
overview: OSMAUS is a product design concept that challenges how project management tools are typically built — most tools are designed for managers who need oversight, not for the people doing the actual work. OSMAUS flips that perspective: it's designed around the individual contributor's day, surfacing only what's relevant right now and keeping everything else one deliberate click away.

The visual language is intentionally architectural — strong grid, minimal chrome, generous whitespace. The UI communicates structure and clarity without feeling sterile or corporate. Every screen was designed to reduce cognitive overhead and protect focus: less context-switching, less searching, more doing.

challenges.challenge: Designing a dashboard that surfaces enough context to be useful without becoming overwhelming.
challenges.solution: Used a dynamic content zone system — the center panel adapts based on the user's active task, collapsing secondary panels automatically. Information density scales with intent, not with data availability.

challenges.challenge: Making task hierarchy and priority visible without relying on color-coded labels or nested indentation.
challenges.solution: Introduced a visual weight system where deadline proximity, priority level, and ownership are communicated through typography scale and opacity — giving the interface a natural reading order without visual noise.

challenges.challenge: Creating a design that felt calm and focused even under heavy project loads.
challenges.solution: Designed a progressive reveal pattern — only active sprint tasks surface in the main view by default. Backlog, archive, and dependencies live in contextual side panels opened on demand, keeping the workspace uncluttered.

projects.title: Brews & Co.
projects.subtitle: A warm, editorial coffee shop website design that blends artisanal identity with digital atmosphere — crafted in Photoshop, rooted in texture and intention.
projects.desc: Coffee shop branding and website design concept — a warm, tactile visual identity paired with an editorial digital presence built entirely in Photoshop.
overview: Brews & Co. is a visual identity and website design concept for an artisanal coffee shop — the kind of place that takes its craft seriously, where the interior feels curated and the menu tells a story. The design goal was direct: make someone want a coffee the moment they land on the page.

Built entirely in Photoshop, the project explores how texture, warmth, and editorial typography can communicate quality and care without a single word of marketing copy. The color palette was pulled directly from roasted bean tones — deep espresso browns, warm caramel, and soft cream — then paired with serif type that feels both timeless and contemporary.

challenges.challenge: Creating atmosphere and warmth through a static, screen-based medium.
challenges.solution: Layered paper textures, soft grain overlays, and warm-toned photo composites to give the design a tactile, almost physical quality — the kind of feeling you'd associate with holding a warm cup in a quiet room.

challenges.challenge: Balancing editorial ambition with practical website needs — menu, ordering, and location.
challenges.solution: Separated the experience into two distinct visual modes: an immersive hero and story section for atmosphere and brand discovery, and a clean high-contrast utility layer for ordering and navigation, so neither mode compromises the other.

challenges.challenge: Establishing a brand identity that stood apart from the generic rustic-vintage café aesthetic.
challenges.solution: Drew inspiration from luxury food editorial rather than artisan nostalgia — treating the coffee itself as the hero through deliberate composition, generous negative space, and a palette that felt premium without being cold.
